title: Clear Nasal Discharge from One Nostril in Dogs: Causes and Triage
meta_desc: Notice clear discharge from one of your dog's nostrils? Learn about the causes, urgency levels, and how a photo helps your vet provide care.
content_html:
Understanding Unilateral Nasal Discharge

When a dog experiences clear, watery discharge from only one nostril—known as unilateral discharge—it typically indicates a localized issue within that specific nasal passage. This is often caused by minor irritants, the early stages of a viral infection, or a small foreign body like a blade of grass or a seed that has become lodged.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for clear nasal discharge from one nostril is Low. If your dog is otherwise acting normally, eating well, and not showing signs of respiratory distress, this is generally not an immediate emergency. You should monitor the situation and schedule a routine veterinary appointment if the discharge persists for more than 24 to 48 hours or if your dog starts sneezing excessively.

Why Taking a Photo Helps

Providing your veterinarian with a clear, high-resolution photo or video of the discharge is an excellent triage tool. It allows the clinical team to see the exact consistency and volume of the fluid, as well as any subtle swelling of the muzzle that might not be apparent during a physical exam if the discharge is intermittent. This visual evidence helps your vet determine the necessity of diagnostic imaging or rhinoscopy.

faqs.question: Can allergies cause discharge in just one nostril?
faqs.answer: While environmental allergies typically affect both nostrils, it is possible for a localized irritant to cause a reaction on only one side.

faqs.question: When should I become more concerned?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary care sooner if the discharge turns yellow, green, or bloody, or if your dog begins pawing at their face and showing signs of pain.

faqs.question: Could there be an object stuck in my dog's nose?
faqs.answer: Yes, unilateral discharge is a common sign of a foreign body. Dogs sniffing in tall grass often inhale seeds or debris that can cause one-sided irritation.
